content: Trust is not a single grand gesture or a sweeping declaration — it is assembled slowly through hundreds of small, consistent moments of follow-through and care. Brown uses the metaphor of a marble jar: every time someone shows up for you in a small way, a marble goes in; every betrayal removes them. The jar fills or empties one marble at a time, never all at once.
implication: Leaders and builders should stop waiting for the 'big moment' to prove trustworthiness and instead audit the daily micro-behaviors — the returned call, the kept confidence, the honest answer — that either fill or drain the jar with every person they lead.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: Brown developed the BRAVING inventory as a way to convert the slippery, abstract noun 'trust' into seven concrete, observable behaviors: Boundaries, Reliability, Accountability, Vault, Integrity, Non-judgment, and Generosity. Rather than asking 'do I trust this person?' — a feeling that is hard to examine — BRAVING asks which specific behaviors are or are not present. This makes trust measurable, repairable, and teachable.
implication: Teams and organizations that want to rebuild or strengthen trust should use BRAVING as a diagnostic — naming specifically which element has been violated rather than treating 'lack of trust' as a vague, unfixable feeling.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: The B in BRAVING stands for Boundaries: you trust someone when you believe they will honor your boundaries and are willing to say no when something doesn't feel right. Critically, trusting someone also requires that you yourself are willing to hold your own boundaries — because you cannot trust someone else to respect limits you have not established or communicated. Boundaries are the foundational condition, not an afterthought.
implication: Leaders who want to be trusted must model clear boundary-setting — for themselves and for their teams. If you never say no, people don't trust your yes either; ambiguity about limits erodes confidence in every dimension of the relationship.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: The R in BRAVING stands for Reliability: doing what you say you will do, consistently and within your actual capacity. This means not over-promising in order to appear generous or committed, and not under-delivering because you were unwilling to say no at the outset. Reliability is degraded not only by breaking commitments but by making more commitments than you can realistically honor.
implication: Founders and leaders who habitually over-commit — from deals to deadlines to cultural promises — are eroding trust even when they intend the opposite. Reliable leadership requires calibrating promises to capacity, not to what people want to hear.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: The A in BRAVING stands for Accountability: owning your mistakes, apologizing, and making amends — and also holding others accountable without blame or shaming. Accountability is a two-directional behavior; it requires you to take responsibility for your own conduct and to expect the same from others without attacking their identity or worth. Brown consistently distinguishes accountability from blame, noting that blame is a way of discharging pain rather than solving problems.
implication: Cultures that confuse accountability with blame punish failure instead of learning from it, destroying psychological safety and trust simultaneously. Real accountability culture requires that leaders go first — modeling the apology and the amends before expecting it from anyone else.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: The V in BRAVING stands for Vault: not sharing information or stories that are not yours to share. The vault is not simply about keeping secrets — it is about recognizing that confidences shared in trust are not currency to be traded for connection or social belonging with a third party. Brown argues that one of the fastest ways people lose trust in another person is by watching them share someone else's private story, because they immediately wonder what is said about them.
implication: In team environments, leaders who gossip, even with the intention of building rapport, are actively signaling that no one's vulnerability is safe with them. The vault is not passive silence — it is an active, daily choice to treat shared information as sacred.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: The I in BRAVING stands for Integrity: choosing courage over comfort, choosing what is right over what is fun or easy, and practicing your values rather than simply professing them. Brown is careful to distinguish integrity from perfection — it is not about being flawless, it is about being willing to hold your values even when doing so is costly or unpopular. The practice of integrity, repeated over time, is what makes a person trustworthy under pressure.
implication: Organizations that espouse values on the wall but abandon them under commercial pressure are not just hypocritical — they are actively destroying the trust of every employee who watches the gap between stated and lived values. Integrity is a behavior, not a brand.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: The N in BRAVING stands for Non-judgment: creating a relationship where both people can ask for help without fear of being judged for what they need or for needing help at all. Non-judgment is bidirectional — it requires that you can struggle without self-judgment and that you extend the same freedom to the other person. Brown identifies the inability to receive help as one of the most corrosive forces in trust relationships, because it creates an imbalance that prevents true reciprocity.
implication: Leaders who signal — even subtly — that needing help is weakness are cutting off the feedback loops that would allow problems to surface before they become catastrophic. Non-judgment as a practice means explicitly rewarding people for raising difficulty early rather than penalizing them for imperfection.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: The G in BRAVING stands for Generosity: consistently assuming the most generous interpretation possible of another person's words, actions, and intent — and explicitly communicating that assumption rather than letting ambiguity fester. Brown grounds this not in naivety but in the recognition that trust requires extending good faith before it is proven, and that most misunderstandings begin with the least charitable interpretation of an ambiguous act.
implication: In high-pressure environments where speed creates miscommunication, leaders who default to generous interpretation and say it aloud — 'I'm assuming you meant X, let me check' — prevent cascading breakdowns in trust that would otherwise harden into permanent relational damage.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: Brown draws a sharp distinction between self-trust and interpersonal trust, arguing that BRAVING applies in both directions. Many people who struggle to trust others are actually struggling to trust themselves — their own judgment, their ability to survive disappointment, or their capacity to set and maintain boundaries. You cannot build durable trust with others if you have not first built it with yourself through consistent internal integrity.
implication: Leaders who chronically second-guess their own decisions, or who abandon their stated values under social pressure, are not just experiencing personal uncertainty — they are actively modeling that values are negotiable, which destroys team-level trust in ways that no external culture initiative can repair.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: Brown illustrates the marble jar metaphor through a story about her daughter's experience with a classmate who violated a confidence. The lesson her daughter learned — and that Brown uses to teach trust — is that trust is not built in the moment of crisis when you most need it; it is built in the ordinary moments long before the crisis arrives. When the crisis comes, you either have enough marbles in the jar or you don't, and there is no shortcut to filling it quickly.
implication: Leaders who invest in small daily acts of trustworthiness — following through on minor promises, acknowledging small contributions, keeping small confidences — are building the relational reserve that will allow their teams to survive the inevitable moments of failure, conflict, and change.
source_ref: The Lesson Brené Brown's Daughter Learned About Trust | SuperSoul Sessions | Oprah Winfrey Network

content: Brown argues that trust is particularly vulnerable to betrayal through four specific behaviors: being disloyal or gossiping, not keeping confidences, lying by omission or commission, and abandoning someone in a moment of vulnerability. What makes these so destructive is that they often happen in small, deniable moments — a comment shared, a story passed on — rather than in dramatic events. The accumulation of small betrayals is as corrosive as a single large one.
implication: Organizations should not wait for a large, visible trust violation to audit their culture. The small daily behaviors — who gets spoken about when they leave the room, whether feedback is given directly or triangulated — are the actual measure of trust health and the site of its repair.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: Brown identifies a common and damaging pattern she calls 'common enemy intimacy' — the construction of false closeness through shared criticism of a third party. It feels like trust because it involves disclosure and mutual vulnerability, but it is actually a vault violation masquerading as bonding. Real trust is not built by tearing others down together; it is built by showing up consistently for each other across time.
implication: Leaders who allow or participate in team cohesion built on shared contempt for other departments, competitors, or absent colleagues are creating a brittle pseudo-trust that will collapse the moment those same people become the absent third party. Sustainable team trust is built through shared values, not shared enemies.
source_ref: Braving the Wilderness

content: Brown consistently frames the BRAVING inventory not just as an assessment tool for others but as a mirror for self-examination. The question is not only whether the people in your life are boundaried, reliable, accountable, vault-keeping, integrated, non-judgmental, and generous toward you — but whether you are those things toward them. Trust is a practice, not a credential, and it requires ongoing self-audit rather than one-time evaluation.
implication: When trust breaks down in a team or relationship, the first BRAVING question is reflexive: which of the seven elements am I not currently modeling? Waiting for the other party to change first is how trust stays broken indefinitely.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: Brown draws a critical link between trust and the willingness to have difficult, direct conversations. The avoidance of hard conversations — through vague feedback, triangulation, or silence — is itself a trust violation because it denies the other person the information they need to show up accountably. Clarity is an act of respect and a structural requirement of trust; without it, even well-intentioned relationships drift into resentment and confusion.
implication: Leaders who soften feedback to the point of unintelligibility, or who discuss performance problems with everyone except the person who needs to hear it, are not being kind — they are withdrawing marbles from the trust jar while telling themselves they are protecting relationships.
source_ref: Brené Brown on How to Lead With Vulnerability at Work | The Interview

content: In contexts of organizational trust, Brown argues that leaders must be willing to be trusted before they can demand it from others. This means being transparent about mistakes, following through on commitments to reports with the same rigor they apply to commitments to executives, and being willing to be vulnerable about uncertainty. Trust flows down before it flows up; the leader who withholds vulnerability while expecting it from their team is asking for something they are not offering.
implication: Senior leaders who want high-trust cultures must audit whether they are applying BRAVING to their own behavior — particularly Reliability and Accountability — with the same scrutiny they would apply to an underperforming direct report. Hierarchical double standards are visible and corrosive.
source_ref: Leading with Authenticity and Vulnerability: Brené Brown & Adam Grant

content: Brown connects the capacity for trust to the broader theme of wholehearted living — arguing that people who struggle to trust others often do so because they have not developed the internal resources to survive the risk of betrayal. The antidote is not more protection but more resilience: building the capacity to be hurt, to recover, and to re-engage rather than building higher walls. Armoring up against betrayal is, in her framing, also armoring up against connection.
implication: Builders and founders who have been burned by betrayal face a genuine choice: armor up and lead with control systems, or develop the resilience to trust again at appropriate scale. The former produces compliance cultures; the latter produces the conditions for innovation, loyalty, and genuine collaboration.
source_ref: Brené Brown - Living With a Whole Heart | Super Soul Sunday S4E03 | Full Episode | OWN

content: Brown uses the concept of 'FFTs' — effing first times — to describe why trust feels so fragile in new relationships or after a rupture. The first time you ask for something new, disclose something vulnerable, or take a risk with someone, it is genuinely terrifying because the marble jar is empty or depleted. She normalizes this discomfort while insisting that it is the only path through — there is no way to fill the jar without tolerating the uncertainty of the first marble.
implication: Leaders rebuilding trust after a breach should not expect the team to feel safe immediately — that expectation compounds the injury. Instead, they should explicitly acknowledge that the jar is empty, commit to the specific BRAVING behaviors that will refill it, and give the process the time it requires without demanding premature forgiveness.
source_ref: Rising Strong

content: Brown emphasizes that the vault element of BRAVING is not just about what you keep private — it is also about what you decline to share about yourself in manipulative or strategic ways. Oversharing personal information to manufacture intimacy or gain social leverage is a vault violation in the opposite direction. Real trust requires calibrated disclosure: sharing what is appropriate to the depth and history of the relationship, not using vulnerability as performance or currency.
implication: Leaders who confuse radical transparency with indiscriminate disclosure — sharing every personal struggle in all-hands meetings, for example — may be seeking connection but are actually undermining the conditions for it. Authentic disclosure serves the relationship; performative disclosure serves the discloser.
source_ref: Brené Brown: Vulnerability, not over-sharing

content: Brown argues that non-judgment — the N in BRAVING — is the element most frequently violated in relationships with people who are otherwise highly trustworthy. A person can be reliable, boundaried, and accountable, and still create an environment where asking for help feels impossible because of subtle signals of judgment about what you need or how you struggle. The judgment doesn't have to be explicit; an eye roll, a tone, or a history of advice-giving instead of listening is enough to close the vault.
implication: Leaders who genuinely want people to bring problems forward early should audit not just their stated response to bad news but their micro-behavioral reaction — the sigh, the furrowed brow, the immediate pivot to solution — that signals judgment before a single word is spoken.
source_ref: Brené Brown on Boundaries, Feelings & Core Emotions | Ten Percent Happier Podcast with Dan Harris

content: Brown addresses the particular pain of trust violations within close relationships — friends, family members, long-term colleagues — where the marble jar has been built over years. A single significant betrayal can empty a full jar with devastating speed, and the pain is proportional to the depth of the investment. The fact that the jar was full does not guarantee it will be refilled; some betrayals are of a type or magnitude that make rebuilding either impossible or unwise.
implication: Leaders and people navigating organizational betrayal should not feel obligated to rebuild every damaged trust relationship. The BRAVING framework helps distinguish between relationships worth repairing — where the specific behaviors can be named, owned, and changed — and those where the pattern is too entrenched or the violation too fundamental.
source_ref: How to Make Peace with People You'll Never Reconcile With | BRENE BROWN

content: Brown connects the generosity element of BRAVING — assuming the most charitable interpretation of ambiguous behavior — to the broader cognitive science of how humans construct stories about others' motives. When someone's behavior is unclear, the brain will automatically generate an explanation, and under stress that explanation defaults to threat. Generosity in trust requires actively interrupting that default narrative and substituting a more generous hypothesis before acting on the initial story.
implication: In remote and asynchronous work environments where context is stripped away from communication, the default-to-threat narrative becomes the dominant relational hazard. Building generous interpretation as an explicit team norm — stating it, modeling it, and rewarding it — is one of the highest-leverage trust interventions available to distributed leaders.
source_ref: Rising Strong

content: Brown's research found that the most common response people have to discovering a lack of trust is to try to fix it through a large gesture — a major apology, a grand restatement of commitment, a public declaration. This almost never works, because trust was not built through large gestures and cannot be restored by them. The only path back is the same path that built it in the first place: small, consistent, observable behaviors repeated over time with no guarantee of outcome.
implication: Leaders attempting to rebuild trust after a significant failure — a broken promise, a public failure of integrity, a moment of abandonment — should resist the impulse toward dramatic redemption narratives and focus instead on the unglamorous, daily micro-behaviors of BRAVING. The jar refills one marble at a time.
source_ref: Brene Brown + Tim Storey - The Anatomy of Trust | Super Soul Sunday S6E03 | Full Episode | OWN
